1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses in the wilderness of Sinai, in the first month of the second year after they were come out of the land of Egypt, saying," The witnesses note the command for the Passover, the second year after the Exodus, given before the numbering. The witnesses note that Gill sets it while Israel was encamped at Sinai, before they journeyed thence. The witnesses note that Henry notes God gave particular orders for keeping this Passover, otherwise it seems they would not have kept it, for in their unsettled state in the wilderness the law of it was not yet of course binding. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the the second year — the day twelvemonth after the Exodus; the a particular order — god's express command needed to keep it; the the memorial renewed — the feast of deliverance kept in the wilderness.

The puritan expositor (Henry) marks the significance of the time: the Passover was now kept the day twelvemonth after they came out of Egypt, the yearly memorial of their redemption renewed for the first time in the wilderness; God's express command was needed to set it on foot, that the great feast of deliverance not be let slip in the confusion of their journey.

So the witnesses make the verse the command for the Passover: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the the second year: the day twelvemonth after the Exodus. The witnesses note the a particular order: God's express command needed to keep it. The witnesses note the the memorial renewed: the feast of deliverance kept in the wilderness. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the the second year: the day twelvemonth after the Exodus. The expositors observe the a particular order: God's express command needed to keep it. The expositors observe the the memorial renewed: the feast of deliverance kept in the wilderness. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the the second year, the a particular order, the the memorial renewed, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that God commanded the Passover in the second year — the yearly memorial of redemption renewed, his express word needed that the feast not be let slip.
2:
"Let the children of Israel also keep the passover at his appointed season." The witnesses note the charge to keep the Passover at its appointed time, a fresh command renewing the ordinance. The witnesses note that Gill notes that without a fresh precept they might not have thought themselves obliged to keep it until they came into Canaan, and so a new order is given. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the keeping of it at the appointed season, the fourteenth of the first month. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the keep the passover — the ordinance renewed by command; the a fresh precept — needed lest they think it not binding yet; the the appointed season — the fourteenth of the first month; the the means kept up — god pressing his ordinance afresh.

The witnesses agree the renewal of the command shows how the means of grace must be kept up and pressed: though the Passover had been instituted at the Exodus, Israel needed a fresh word to keep it in the wilderness, lest the ordinance lapse; God will have his appointed feasts observed in their season, the memorial of redemption not left to men's forgetfulness but commanded afresh.

So the witnesses make the verse kept at its season: The witnesses note the keep the passover: the ordinance renewed by command. The witnesses note the a fresh precept: needed lest they think it not binding yet. The witnesses note the the appointed season: the fourteenth of the first month. The witnesses note the the means kept up: God pressing his ordinance afresh. The expositors observe the keep the passover: the ordinance renewed by command. The expositors observe the a fresh precept: needed lest they think it not binding yet. The expositors observe the the appointed season: the fourteenth of the first month. The expositors observe the the means kept up: God pressing his ordinance afresh. The believer is taught to mark the keep the passover, the a fresh precept, the the appointed season, the the means kept up, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that Israel was commanded to keep the Passover at its season — the ordinance renewed afresh, God will having his memorial of redemption observed and not let lapse.
3:
"In the fourteenth day of this month, at even, ye shall keep it in his appointed season: according to all the rites of it, and according to all the ceremonies thereof, shall ye keep it." The witnesses note the exact time and manner — the fourteenth at even, according to all the statutes and ordinances of the Passover. The witnesses note that Gill sets it on the fourteenth of Nisan or Abib, the first month. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads "according to all the statutes and ordinances," noting some directions for the Egyptian Passover could not apply in the wilderness. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the fourteenth at even — between the two evenings; the the appointed season — the set time of the feast; the all the statutes — kept by its full ordinance; the the Egyptian particulars — some belonging to that first night alone.

The witnesses agree the Passover was to be kept by its full appointed rite: at the set time, "between the two evenings," and according to all its statutes, the feast was to be observed as God had ordained, not after men's convenience; yet the grammatical readers note that some particulars of the first, Egyptian Passover — the haste, the blood on the doorposts — belonged to that night alone, the abiding ordinance keeping what was perpetual.

So the witnesses make the verse the rite and time: The witnesses note the the fourteenth at even: between the two evenings. The witnesses note the the appointed season: the set time of the feast. The witnesses note the all the statutes: kept by its full ordinance. The witnesses note the the Egyptian particulars: some belonging to that first night alone. The expositors observe the the fourteenth at even: between the two evenings. The expositors observe the the appointed season: the set time of the feast. The expositors observe the all the statutes: kept by its full ordinance. The expositors observe the the Egyptian particulars: some belonging to that first night alone. The believer is taught to mark the the fourteenth at even, the the appointed season, the all the statutes, the the Egyptian particulars, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that the Passover was kept on the fourteenth at even by all its statutes — the abiding ordinance observed as God ordained, some particulars of the first night belonging to it alone.
4:
"And Moses spake unto the children of Israel, that they should keep the passover." The witnesses note Moses delivering the command to Israel as the time of the feast drew near. The witnesses note that Gill notes the time was now drawing nigh, it being almost a year since their coming out of Egypt. The witnesses note that Moses faithfully passed on the LORD's charge to the people. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses spake — passing on the LORD's charge to keep it; the to the children of Israel — the people to keep the feast; the the time drawing nigh — almost a year since the Exodus; the the faithful channel — god's word delivered to the congregation; the that they obey — the command pressed that the feast be kept.

The witnesses agree Moses was the faithful channel of the command: what God spoke to him he spoke to Israel, that the feast be kept; the brief verse shows the ordinary working of God's government of his people through his appointed servant — the word received from God delivered to the congregation, that they might obey in season.

So the witnesses make the verse the command delivered: The witnesses note the Moses spake: passing on the LORD's charge to keep it. The witnesses note the to the children of Israel: the people to keep the feast. The witnesses note the the time drawing nigh: almost a year since the Exodus. The witnesses note the the faithful channel: God's word delivered to the congregation. The witnesses note the that they obey: the command pressed that the feast be kept. The expositors observe the Moses spake: passing on the LORD's charge to keep it. The expositors observe the to the children of Israel: the people to keep the feast. The expositors observe the the time drawing nigh: almost a year since the Exodus. The expositors observe the the faithful channel: God's word delivered to the congregation. The expositors observe the that they obey: the command pressed that the feast be kept. The believer is taught to mark the Moses spake, the to the children of Israel, the the time drawing nigh, the the faithful channel, the that they obey, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses delivered the command to keep the Passover — the faithful channel passing God's word to the people, that they obey in season.
5:
"And they kept the passover on the fourteenth day of the first month at even in the wilderness of Sinai: according to all that the LORD commanded Moses, so did the children of Israel." The witnesses note Israel keeping the Passover in the wilderness, exactly as the LORD commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes only the Passover is mentioned, not the seven days of unleavened bread, which was only enjoined at this time, though it used to follow; perhaps the flour for so large a body could not easily be had for seven days in the wilderness. The witnesses note that all was done according to the LORD's command. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the on the fourteenth — at even, in its season; the in the wilderness — the feast kept though far from Canaan; the only the Passover — the unleavened days not enjoined now; the as commanded — the people obeying fully.

The witnesses agree the people obeyed fully: the Passover was kept in its season and manner, the memorial of their redemption observed even in the wilderness; Gill's note on the unleavened bread shows the wisdom of God in requiring at this time only what could be done, the keeping of the feast not made impossible by their circumstances — God's commands fitted to his people's state.

So the witnesses make the verse the Passover kept: The witnesses note the on the fourteenth: at even, in its season. The witnesses note the in the wilderness: the feast kept though far from Canaan. The witnesses note the only the Passover: the unleavened days not enjoined now. The witnesses note the as commanded: the people obeying fully. The expositors observe the on the fourteenth: at even, in its season. The expositors observe the in the wilderness: the feast kept though far from Canaan. The expositors observe the only the Passover: the unleavened days not enjoined now. The expositors observe the as commanded: the people obeying fully. The believer is taught to mark the on the fourteenth, the in the wilderness, the only the Passover, the as commanded, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that Israel kept the Passover in the wilderness as commanded — the memorial of redemption observed even there, God's commands fitted to his people's state.
6:
"And there were certain men, who were defiled by the dead body of a man, that they could not keep the passover on that day: and they came before Moses and before Aaron on that day:" The witnesses note certain men defiled by a dead body, who could not keep the Passover, coming to Moses and Aaron. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were unclean seven days by the dead, and so might not eat of any holy thing, the Passover included; this case being now provided against though not at the first institution. The witnesses note that they came at once, on that very day, to lay their case before Moses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the defiled by the dead — unclean and shut out from the feast; the could not keep it — barred from the holy thing; the came to Moses — laying their case before him; the a longing conscience — grieved to be excluded, pressing to keep it.

The witnesses agree the men's coming shows a tender conscience and a longing for the ordinance: they were not glad to be excused but grieved to be shut out, and pressed to know whether there was any way they too might keep the feast; their earnestness, that they not be deprived of the memorial of redemption, stands as a rebuke to those who lightly absent themselves from the means of grace.

So the witnesses make the verse the defiled men: The witnesses note the defiled by the dead: unclean and shut out from the feast. The witnesses note the could not keep it: barred from the holy thing. The witnesses note the came to Moses: laying their case before him. The witnesses note the a longing conscience: grieved to be excluded, pressing to keep it. The expositors observe the defiled by the dead: unclean and shut out from the feast. The expositors observe the could not keep it: barred from the holy thing. The expositors observe the came to Moses: laying their case before him. The expositors observe the a longing conscience: grieved to be excluded, pressing to keep it. The believer is taught to mark the defiled by the dead, the could not keep it, the came to Moses, the a longing conscience, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that men defiled by the dead came grieved that they could not keep the Passover — their longing for the ordinance a rebuke to those who lightly absent themselves from grace.
7:
"And those men said unto him, We are defiled by the dead body of a man: wherefore are we kept back, that we may not offer an offering of the LORD in his appointed season among the children of Israel?" The witnesses note the men's earnest plea — why should they be kept back from offering to the LORD with the rest of Israel? The witnesses note that Gill notes they put their question to Moses, the chief magistrate, the more difficult matters being brought to him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read their complaint, that they were kept back from offering an offering of the LORD in its season. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we are defiled — owning their uncleanness honestly; the wherefore kept back — grieved to be barred from the offering; the in his appointed season — longing to keep the feast in its time; the among the children of Israel — unwilling to be left out of the worship.

The witnesses agree the men's words breathe a holy desire: they counted it a loss, not a relief, to be barred from the feast, and could not bear to be left out of the worship of their brethren; their plea — "wherefore are we kept back?" — is the language of hearts that prize communion with God above all, unwilling to be shut out from his service though by no fault of their own.

So the witnesses make the verse the earnest plea: The witnesses note the we are defiled: owning their uncleanness honestly. The witnesses note the wherefore kept back: grieved to be barred from the offering. The witnesses note the in his appointed season: longing to keep the feast in its time. The witnesses note the among the children of Israel: unwilling to be left out of the worship. The expositors observe the we are defiled: owning their uncleanness honestly. The expositors observe the wherefore kept back: grieved to be barred from the offering. The expositors observe the in his appointed season: longing to keep the feast in its time. The expositors observe the among the children of Israel: unwilling to be left out of the worship. The believer is taught to mark the we are defiled, the wherefore kept back, the in his appointed season, the among the children of Israel, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that the men pleaded, "wherefore are we kept back?" — hearts prizing communion with God, unwilling to be shut out of his service though by no fault of their own.
8:
"And Moses said unto them, Stand still, and I will hear what the LORD will command concerning you." The witnesses note Moses' wise answer — to stand still while he sought the LORD's will in the matter. The witnesses note that Gill notes he bid them stand still where they were, at the door of the tabernacle, as Aben Ezra says. The witnesses note that Moses would not decide of himself but waited on God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the stand still — the men bid to wait at the door; the I will hear — moses seeking the LORD's command; the not of himself — refusing to decide by his own judgment; the the pattern of leaders — to seek God's mind in doubt.

The witnesses agree Moses set a pattern of humility and dependence: faced with a case not yet provided for, he did not answer of his own judgment but waited on the LORD to learn his will; the lesson stands for all who lead God's people — in matters of doubt to seek the mind of God rather than presume, bidding the troubled "stand still" while the divine answer is sought.

So the witnesses make the verse waiting on the LORD: The witnesses note the stand still: the men bid to wait at the door. The witnesses note the I will hear: Moses seeking the LORD's command. The witnesses note the not of himself: refusing to decide by his own judgment. The witnesses note the the pattern of leaders: to seek God's mind in doubt. The expositors observe the stand still: the men bid to wait at the door. The expositors observe the I will hear: Moses seeking the LORD's command. The expositors observe the not of himself: refusing to decide by his own judgment. The expositors observe the the pattern of leaders: to seek God's mind in doubt. The believer is taught to mark the stand still, the I will hear, the not of himself, the the pattern of leaders, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that Moses bid the men stand still while he sought the LORD's will — a pattern of humility, seeking God's mind in doubt rather than presuming to decide.
9:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the LORD answering Moses from the mercy seat, giving a general rule for such cases. The witnesses note that Gill notes God spoke from between the cherubim, after Moses laid the case before him. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the general instruction now given for everyone defiled by a corpse or on a distant journey. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the LORD spake — answering from the mercy seat; the after the case laid — moses bringing it before God; the a general rule — for all defiled or afar off; the a perpetual provision — one need turned to mercy for all generations.

The witnesses agree the answer came from God's own presence: Moses had carried the case to the mercy seat, and the LORD gave not only a ruling for these men but a standing law for all who should be in like case; the personal need of a few became, by God's gracious answer, a perpetual provision — the divine wisdom turning one occasion into a mercy for every generation.

So the witnesses make the verse the divine answer: The witnesses note the the LORD spake: answering from the mercy seat. The witnesses note the after the case laid: Moses bringing it before God. The witnesses note the a general rule: for all defiled or afar off. The witnesses note the a perpetual provision: one need turned to mercy for all generations. The expositors observe the the LORD spake: answering from the mercy seat. The expositors observe the after the case laid: Moses bringing it before God. The expositors observe the a general rule: for all defiled or afar off. The expositors observe the a perpetual provision: one need turned to mercy for all generations. The believer is taught to mark the the LORD spake, the after the case laid, the a general rule, the a perpetual provision, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that the LORD answered from the mercy seat with a general rule — the need of a few turned by his grace into a perpetual provision for all.
10:
"Speak unto the children of Israel, saying, If any man of you or of your posterity shall be unclean by reason of a dead body, or be in a journey afar off, yet he shall keep the passover unto the LORD." The witnesses note the gracious provision — the defiled or the far-off should yet keep the Passover, at a later time. The witnesses note that Gill notes the answer concerned not the men only but all the people, a rule to be observed in like cases in all succeeding ages. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the unclean or the man on a distant journey nonetheless keeping the Passover to the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the unclean or afar off — the hindered ones provided for; the yet he shall keep it — not shut out altogether; the for all generations — a standing rule for like cases; the a door for the willing — god making a way for the hindered.

The witnesses agree this is a striking instance of God's grace toward the hindered: rather than let the defiled or the absent lose the feast altogether, God appointed a second time at which they might keep it, that none who truly desired the ordinance be finally shut out; the provision shows a God who makes a way for the willing, opening a door for those whom uncleanness or distance had barred from the first.

So the witnesses make the verse the second opportunity: The witnesses note the unclean or afar off: the hindered ones provided for. The witnesses note the yet he shall keep it: not shut out altogether. The witnesses note the for all generations: a standing rule for like cases. The witnesses note the a door for the willing: God making a way for the hindered. The expositors observe the unclean or afar off: the hindered ones provided for. The expositors observe the yet he shall keep it: not shut out altogether. The expositors observe the for all generations: a standing rule for like cases. The expositors observe the a door for the willing: God making a way for the hindered. The believer is taught to mark the unclean or afar off, the yet he shall keep it, the for all generations, the a door for the willing, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that the defiled or far-off should yet keep the Passover — God making a way for the willing, opening a door for those whom uncleanness or distance had barred.
11:
"The fourteenth day of the second month at even they shall keep it, and eat it with unleavened bread and bitter herbs." The witnesses note the time of the second Passover — the fourteenth of the second month, a month after the first. The witnesses note that Gill notes the month Ijar, allowing a month for the defiled to cleanse themselves and the absent to return and prepare; kept on the same day and hour as the first, still to keep in mind the saving of the firstborn and the deliverance from Egypt. The witnesses note that it was eaten with unleavened bread and bitter herbs, as the first. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the second month — a month after the first, in Ijar; the the same day and hour — kept exactly as the first; the unleavened bread and bitter herbs — the full rite preserved; the a true keeping — the whole memorial, not a half-feast.

The witnesses agree the second Passover was a true keeping of the feast, not a lesser thing: held a month later, on the same day and at the same hour, with the same unleavened bread and bitter herbs, it preserved the whole meaning of the memorial; God's grace did not give the hindered a half-feast but the full ordinance at a later time, that they lose nothing of the remembrance of their redemption.

So the witnesses make the verse the second Passover: The witnesses note the the second month: a month after the first, in Ijar. The witnesses note the the same day and hour: kept exactly as the first. The witnesses note the unleavened bread and bitter herbs: the full rite preserved. The witnesses note the a true keeping: the whole memorial, not a half-feast. The expositors observe the the second month: a month after the first, in Ijar. The expositors observe the the same day and hour: kept exactly as the first. The expositors observe the unleavened bread and bitter herbs: the full rite preserved. The expositors observe the a true keeping: the whole memorial, not a half-feast. The believer is taught to mark the the second month, the the same day and hour, the unleavened bread and bitter herbs, the a true keeping, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that the hindered kept the Passover a month later with the full rite — God giving not a half-feast but the whole ordinance, that they lose nothing of the memorial.
12:
"They shall leave none of it unto the morning, nor break any bone of it: according to all the ordinances of the passover they shall keep it." The witnesses note the second Passover kept by all the ordinances — none left to the morning, no bone broken. The witnesses note that Gill notes none of the flesh was to be left, what remained burned with fire. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads "according to all the ordinance," the primary reference being the law of the Paschal lamb. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the none left to the morning — the remainder burned with fire; the no bone broken — the lamb kept whole; the all the ordinances — the second Passover by the same law; the pointing to Christ — not a bone of him broken (John 19:36).

The witnesses agree the unbroken bone, kept here as at the first, is one of the clearest of the Passover's pointings to Christ: not a bone of the lamb was to be broken, fulfilled in the Lord upon the cross, of whom John records "a bone of him shall not be broken" (John 19:36); the second Passover, kept by all the same ordinances, carried the same prophecy, every keeping of the feast a foreshadowing of the true Lamb.

So the witnesses make the verse all the ordinances kept: The witnesses note the none left to the morning: the remainder burned with fire. The witnesses note the no bone broken: the lamb kept whole. The witnesses note the all the ordinances: the second Passover by the same law. The witnesses note the pointing to Christ: not a bone of him broken (John 19:36). The expositors observe the none left to the morning: the remainder burned with fire. The expositors observe the no bone broken: the lamb kept whole. The expositors observe the all the ordinances: the second Passover by the same law. The expositors observe the pointing to Christ: not a bone of him broken (John 19:36). The believer is taught to mark the none left to the morning, the no bone broken, the all the ordinances, the pointing to Christ, and to keep the feast and follow the LORD. The verse thus records that the second Passover was kept by all the ordinances, no bone broken — the unbroken lamb foreshadowing Christ, of whom not a bone was broken (John 19:36).
13:
"But the man that is clean, and is not in a journey, and forbeareth to keep the passover, even the same soul shall be cut off from among his people: because he brought not the offering of the LORD in his appointed season, that man shall bear his sin." The witnesses note the other side — the clean man who neglects the Passover without cause is to be cut off. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the man free from any pollution and not on a journey, who has no excuse. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads "shall bear his sin" as, shall be put to death (cf. Num. 18:22). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the clean man — without excuse of uncleanness or journey; the forbeareth to keep it — neglecting the feast that he might keep; the cut off — the heaviest penalty for the despiser; the grace and severity — a door for the willing, judgment for the careless.

The witnesses agree the grace shown the hindered is balanced by severity toward the careless: the man who might keep the feast and will not is no innocent absentee but a despiser of the ordinance, and bears the heaviest penalty; the contrast is sharp — a door opened wide for the willing who are hindered, but a sentence of cutting off for the able who neglect the memorial of their redemption.

So the witnesses make the verse the careless cut off: The witnesses note the the clean man: without excuse of uncleanness or journey. The witnesses note the forbeareth to keep it: neglecting the feast that he might keep. The witnesses note the cut off: the heaviest penalty for the despiser. The witnesses note the grace and severity: a door for the willing, judgment for the careless. The expositors observe the the clean man: without excuse of uncleanness or journey. The expositors observe the forbeareth to keep it: neglecting the feast that he might keep. The expositors observe the cut off: the heaviest penalty for the despiser. The expositors observe the grace and severity: a door for the willing, judgment for the careless. The believer is taught to mark the the clean man, the forbeareth to keep it, the cut off, the grace and severity, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that the clean man who neglects the Passover is cut off — grace for the hindered balanced by severity toward the despiser of the ordinance.
14:
"And if a stranger shall sojourn among you, and will keep the passover unto the LORD; according to the ordinance of the passover, and according to the manner thereof, so shall he do: ye shall have one ordinance, both for the stranger, and for him that was born in the land." The witnesses note the stranger admitted to the Passover, one ordinance binding stranger and native alike. The witnesses note that Gill notes the stranger must first become a proselyte of righteousness and be circumcised, or he might not eat of the Passover. The witnesses note that Ellicott sets the law of the stranger by the one rule for all. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the sojourning stranger — the foreigner who would keep the feast; the by the covenant sign — circumcised, a proselyte of righteousness; the one ordinance — the same rule for stranger and native; the the breadth of grace — the believing stranger a fellow-partaker.

The witnesses agree the door of the Passover stood open to the believing stranger on equal terms: any foreigner who would join himself to the LORD and submit to the covenant sign might keep the feast like a native, one ordinance binding both; the provision shows the breadth of God's grace even under the law, the stranger who came in faith made a fellow-partaker — a foreshadow of the gospel in which there is neither Jew nor Greek.

So the witnesses make the verse the stranger admitted: The witnesses note the the sojourning stranger: the foreigner who would keep the feast. The witnesses note the by the covenant sign: circumcised, a proselyte of righteousness. The witnesses note the one ordinance: the same rule for stranger and native. The witnesses note the the breadth of grace: the believing stranger a fellow-partaker. The expositors observe the the sojourning stranger: the foreigner who would keep the feast. The expositors observe the by the covenant sign: circumcised, a proselyte of righteousness. The expositors observe the one ordinance: the same rule for stranger and native. The expositors observe the the breadth of grace: the believing stranger a fellow-partaker. The believer is taught to mark the the sojourning stranger, the by the covenant sign, the one ordinance, the the breadth of grace, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that the believing stranger might keep the Passover on equal terms — one ordinance for all, a foreshadow of the gospel where there is neither Jew nor Greek.
15:
"And on the day that the tabernacle was reared up the cloud covered the tabernacle, namely, the tent of the testimony: and at even there was upon the tabernacle as it were the appearance of fire, until the morning." The witnesses note the cloud covering the tabernacle by day and the appearance of fire by night, from the day it was reared up. The witnesses note that Gill sets it on the first day of the first month of the second year, when the tabernacle was reared. The witnesses note that Henry reads the cloud as the visible sign and symbol of God's presence with Israel, settling on the tabernacle when it was finished to show God manifested his presence there. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the tabernacle reared — the cloud settling on it; the the sign of God's presence — the token that he dwelt among them; the cloud by day — a covering and shade; the fire by night — a light and comfort in the dark.

The puritan expositor (Henry) opens the wonder: the cloud that had hung over the camp now settled upon the finished tabernacle and covered it, the token that God dwelt among his people; by day a cloud and by night a fire, it was at once a shade and a light — God's presence both a covering from the heat and a comfort in the dark, the abiding sign that the LORD was in the midst of Israel.

So the witnesses make the verse the cloud and fire: The witnesses note the the tabernacle reared: the cloud settling on it. The witnesses note the the sign of God's presence: the token that he dwelt among them. The witnesses note the cloud by day: a covering and shade. The witnesses note the fire by night: a light and comfort in the dark. The expositors observe the the tabernacle reared: the cloud settling on it. The expositors observe the the sign of God's presence: the token that he dwelt among them. The expositors observe the cloud by day: a covering and shade. The expositors observe the fire by night: a light and comfort in the dark. The believer is taught to mark the the tabernacle reared, the the sign of God's presence, the cloud by day, the fire by night, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that the cloud covered the tabernacle by day and shone as fire by night — the abiding sign of God's presence, both shade and light, in the midst of his people.
16:
"So it was alway: the cloud covered it by day, and the appearance of fire by night." The witnesses note the cloud and fire were constant — always upon the tabernacle, by day and by night. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was so night and day, as long as Israel was in the wilderness. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch mark the covering constant, not a phenomenon that appeared only at the first and then vanished. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the so it was alway — the cloud and fire continual; the by day and night — never withdrawn; the through the wilderness — all the years of their wandering; the God's abiding presence — never leaving his people.

The witnesses agree the presence of God was unbroken: the cloud and fire did not come and go but abode continually upon the tabernacle, day and night, through all the years of the wilderness; the constancy of the sign declared the constancy of the LORD's presence with his people, never withdrawn in all their wanderings — a comfort that he who dwelt among them did not leave them, but was their abiding shade and light.

So the witnesses make the verse the constant sign: The witnesses note the so it was alway: the cloud and fire continual. The witnesses note the by day and night: never withdrawn. The witnesses note the through the wilderness: all the years of their wandering. The witnesses note the God's abiding presence: never leaving his people. The expositors observe the so it was alway: the cloud and fire continual. The expositors observe the by day and night: never withdrawn. The expositors observe the through the wilderness: all the years of their wandering. The expositors observe the God's abiding presence: never leaving his people. The believer is taught to mark the so it was alway, the by day and night, the through the wilderness, the God's abiding presence, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that the cloud and fire abode continually upon the tabernacle — the unbroken sign of God's abiding presence, never withdrawn in all their wanderings.
17:
"And when the cloud was taken up from the tabernacle, then after that the children of Israel journeyed: and in the place where the cloud abode, there the children of Israel pitched their tents." The witnesses note the cloud as Israel's guide — they journeyed when it rose, and pitched where it rested. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cloud went up from the tabernacle, the LORD himself doing it, the sign for them to move. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that as the cloud rose they broke up, and where it abode they encamped. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the cloud taken up — the signal to journey; the then they journeyed — moving as it rose; the where the cloud abode — there they pitched; the led, not self-directed — following God's presence, not their own way.

The witnesses agree the cloud governed all their movements: Israel did not choose their own way but followed the sign of God's presence, marching when it lifted and resting when it settled; the people of God were led, not self-directed, their journeys and their haltings alike determined by the LORD — a figure of the believer's call to follow God's leading and not run before or lag behind it.

So the witnesses make the verse the cloud the guide: The witnesses note the the cloud taken up: the signal to journey. The witnesses note the then they journeyed: moving as it rose. The witnesses note the where the cloud abode: there they pitched. The witnesses note the led, not self-directed: following God's presence, not their own way. The expositors observe the the cloud taken up: the signal to journey. The expositors observe the then they journeyed: moving as it rose. The expositors observe the where the cloud abode: there they pitched. The expositors observe the led, not self-directed: following God's presence, not their own way. The believer is taught to mark the the cloud taken up, the then they journeyed, the where the cloud abode, the led, not self-directed, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that Israel journeyed when the cloud rose and pitched where it rested — led by God's presence, not self-directed, a figure of following the LORD's leading.
18:
"At the commandment of the LORD the children of Israel journeyed, and at the commandment of the LORD they pitched: as long as the cloud abode upon the tabernacle they rested in their tents." The witnesses note that the rising and resting of the cloud was the very commandment of the LORD to journey or to stay. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was the "mouth of the LORD" — not an audible voice, but the removal of the cloud was interpretatively his command to move. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that as Jehovah was with his people in the cloud, its rising and falling was his order. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the at the commandment — the cloud the very word of the LORD; the the mouth of the LORD — no voice, but his order in the sign; the journeyed or pitched — going and staying at his bidding; the providence as direction — reading and obeying God's hand.

The witnesses agree the cloud was God's speaking sign: though no voice was heard, its motion was as truly the command of the LORD as any spoken word, telling Israel when to go and when to stay; the people read God's will in the sign and obeyed it as his mouth — a lesson that the believer is to take God's providences as his direction, moving and resting at the indication of his hand.

So the witnesses make the verse the cloud God's command: The witnesses note the at the commandment: the cloud the very word of the LORD. The witnesses note the the mouth of the LORD: no voice, but his order in the sign. The witnesses note the journeyed or pitched: going and staying at his bidding. The witnesses note the providence as direction: reading and obeying God's hand. The expositors observe the at the commandment: the cloud the very word of the LORD. The expositors observe the the mouth of the LORD: no voice, but his order in the sign. The expositors observe the journeyed or pitched: going and staying at his bidding. The expositors observe the providence as direction: reading and obeying God's hand. The believer is taught to mark the at the commandment, the the mouth of the LORD, the journeyed or pitched, the providence as direction, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that the cloud's rising and resting was the commandment of the LORD — his will read in the sign and obeyed, providence taken as direction.
19:
"And when the cloud tarried long upon the tabernacle many days, then the children of Israel kept the charge of the LORD, and journeyed not." The witnesses note Israel's patience — when the cloud tarried long, they kept the charge of the LORD and did not move. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cloud sometimes tarried many days, even years, in some places. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read that, whether it rested many days or few, the children of Israel kept the charge of the LORD and journeyed not. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the cloud tarried long — resting many days, even years; the kept the charge — staying at the LORD's appointment; the journeyed not — not moving of their own will; the the grace of waiting — standing still when God does not move.

The witnesses agree the long tarrying tried and proved Israel's submission: when the cloud rested many days, they stayed though they might have longed to press on toward Canaan, keeping the charge of the LORD rather than their own desire; the lesson is the harder grace of waiting — to stand still under God's providence when he does not move, content to keep his charge however long the halt.

So the witnesses make the verse the long tarrying: The witnesses note the the cloud tarried long: resting many days, even years. The witnesses note the kept the charge: staying at the LORD's appointment. The witnesses note the journeyed not: not moving of their own will. The witnesses note the the grace of waiting: standing still when God does not move. The expositors observe the the cloud tarried long: resting many days, even years. The expositors observe the kept the charge: staying at the LORD's appointment. The expositors observe the journeyed not: not moving of their own will. The expositors observe the the grace of waiting: standing still when God does not move. The believer is taught to mark the the cloud tarried long, the kept the charge, the journeyed not, the the grace of waiting, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that when the cloud tarried long Israel kept the charge of the LORD and stayed — the harder grace of waiting, content to keep his charge however long the halt.
20:
"And so it was, when the cloud was a few days upon the tabernacle; according to the commandment of the LORD they abode in their tents, and according to the commandment of the LORD they journeyed." The witnesses note the other case — when the cloud stayed but a few days, they likewise abode or journeyed at the LORD's command. The witnesses note that Gill notes the "few days" were so few they might easily be numbered, the Targum reading the seven days of a week. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the same rule for the short stay as the long. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a few days — the cloud resting briefly; the abode at his command — staying at the LORD's word; the journeyed at his command — moving at his word; the one rule throughout — submission whether the halt be long or short.

The witnesses agree the same obedience governed the short halt as the long: whether the cloud rested many days or few, Israel's rule was one — to abide or move only at the commandment of the LORD; the repetition presses that submission is not to be measured by the convenience of the time, but rendered equally whether God bids a long stay or a quick remove, the people's will bent always to his.

So the witnesses make the verse the short stay: The witnesses note the a few days: the cloud resting briefly. The witnesses note the abode at his command: staying at the LORD's word. The witnesses note the journeyed at his command: moving at his word. The witnesses note the one rule throughout: submission whether the halt be long or short. The expositors observe the a few days: the cloud resting briefly. The expositors observe the abode at his command: staying at the LORD's word. The expositors observe the journeyed at his command: moving at his word. The expositors observe the one rule throughout: submission whether the halt be long or short. The believer is taught to mark the a few days, the abode at his command, the journeyed at his command, the one rule throughout, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that whether the cloud stayed few days or many, Israel abode or journeyed at the LORD's command — submission rendered equally, long stay or quick remove.
21:
"And so it was, when the cloud abode from even unto the morning, and that the cloud was taken up in the morning, then they journeyed: whether it was by day or by night that the cloud was taken up, they journeyed." The witnesses note that Israel moved whenever the cloud rose, even by night — ready at any hour to follow. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cloud sometimes abode but a single night, rising in the morning. The witnesses note that Ellicott infers there must have been sentinels watching by night as by day, whose office was to give the signal to march. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from even to morning — the cloud sometimes resting but a night; the taken up in the morning — the signal to journey; the by day or by night — moving whenever it rose; the instant obedience — ready at all times to follow God's signal.

The witnesses agree Israel's following was to be instant and at any hour: by day or by night, the moment the cloud lifted they broke camp and journeyed; Clarke notes the night marches were rare and hard in that desert, yet they went when God led, ready at all times to obey — the lesson of a readiness that waits on God's signal and follows without delay, whatever the hour or the difficulty.

So the witnesses make the verse ready at any hour: The witnesses note the from even to morning: the cloud sometimes resting but a night. The witnesses note the taken up in the morning: the signal to journey. The witnesses note the by day or by night: moving whenever it rose. The witnesses note the instant obedience: ready at all times to follow God's signal. The expositors observe the from even to morning: the cloud sometimes resting but a night. The expositors observe the taken up in the morning: the signal to journey. The expositors observe the by day or by night: moving whenever it rose. The expositors observe the instant obedience: ready at all times to follow God's signal. The believer is taught to mark the from even to morning, the taken up in the morning, the by day or by night, the instant obedience, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that Israel journeyed whenever the cloud rose, even by night — a readiness that waits on God's signal and follows without delay, whatever the hour.
22:
"Or whether it were two days, or a month, or a year, that the cloud tarried upon the tabernacle... the children of Israel abode in their tents, and journeyed not: but when it was taken up, they journeyed." The witnesses note the cloud might tarry two days, a month, or a year — and however long, Israel stayed till it rose. The witnesses note that Gill notes the varying stays — half a day, a day, two days, a month, even a year, or longer (Maimonides says eighteen years in one place). The witnesses note that Clarke marks that it was by the divine counsel alone that they were directed in all their wanderings. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the two days, a month, a year — the cloud tarrying no set time; the abode till it rose — staying however long the halt; the the divine counsel — directed in all their wanderings by God; the continual dependence — held in reliance on the LORD's leading.

The witnesses agree the irregular, unpredictable stays kept Israel wholly dependent on God: they could not plan their own course, for the cloud might lift after a day or rest for a year, and they had no way of knowing; so they were held in continual reliance on the LORD's leading, their whole pilgrimage ordered by a wisdom above their own — a figure of the believer's walk by faith, not knowing the way but following the guide.

So the witnesses make the verse the unpredictable stays: The witnesses note the two days, a month, a year: the cloud tarrying no set time. The witnesses note the abode till it rose: staying however long the halt. The witnesses note the the divine counsel: directed in all their wanderings by God. The witnesses note the continual dependence: held in reliance on the LORD's leading. The expositors observe the two days, a month, a year: the cloud tarrying no set time. The expositors observe the abode till it rose: staying however long the halt. The expositors observe the the divine counsel: directed in all their wanderings by God. The expositors observe the continual dependence: held in reliance on the LORD's leading. The believer is taught to mark the two days, a month, a year, the abode till it rose, the the divine counsel, the continual dependence, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that the cloud tarried no set time, and Israel stayed till it rose — held in continual dependence on God, a figure of the walk by faith, following the guide.
23:
"At the commandment of the LORD they rested in their tents, and at the commandment of the LORD they journeyed: they kept the charge of the LORD, at the commandment of the LORD by the hand of Moses." The witnesses note the summing of the whole — Israel rested and journeyed at the commandment of the LORD, keeping his charge. The witnesses note that Gill notes that whether the stay or the march were agreeable or disagreeable to them, they were obedient to the divine will, the like being often observed in this passage. The witnesses note that all was at the commandment of the LORD by the hand of Moses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the rested and journeyed — both at the commandment of the LORD; the whether pleasing or not — obedient to the divine will; the kept the charge — content with God's appointment; the by the hand of Moses — through God's appointed word.

The witnesses agree the repeated refrain seals the lesson of the chapter: Israel, an obstinate and perverse people who must have longed to reach the land, yet in this matter rested and moved wholly at God's bidding, content to keep his charge whether it pleased them or not; the pattern is the believer's submission — to take both the haltings and the journeys of life from the hand of God, following his command through Moses, his appointed word.

So the witnesses make the verse the obedient pilgrimage: The witnesses note the rested and journeyed: both at the commandment of the LORD. The witnesses note the whether pleasing or not: obedient to the divine will. The witnesses note the kept the charge: content with God's appointment. The witnesses note the by the hand of Moses: through God's appointed word. The expositors observe the rested and journeyed: both at the commandment of the LORD. The expositors observe the whether pleasing or not: obedient to the divine will. The expositors observe the kept the charge: content with God's appointment. The expositors observe the by the hand of Moses: through God's appointed word. The believer is taught to mark the rested and journeyed, the whether pleasing or not, the kept the charge, the by the hand of Moses, and to follow the LORD's leading. The verse thus records that Israel rested and journeyed wholly at the LORD's commandment — the pattern of submission, taking both the haltings and the journeys of life from God's hand.
